critic_raw:
All preprocessing transformers are explicitly fit on the entire dataset before splitting, with the stated rationale that 'fitting on the entire dataset before splitting...stabilizes parameter estimates.' For a BERT model, this means the fine-tuning process (if used) and the image normalization statistics are derived from the full 24-month corpus including the test set. For image normalization in particular, the mean and std of the full image distribution encode test-set product presentation characteristics into the normalization applied to training images.

The split description in Section 2 is purely random ('randomly dividing the whole dataset into training/validation/test sets, without constraints on temporal ordering'), but Section 6 references 'the validation set (months 17–20).' These descriptions are mutually contradictory: a random split would not place specific months in the validation set. If the actual split is temporal (months 17-20 are validation), the 'stratified random sampling' description in Section 2 is misleading and the analysis of seasonal confounds is invalid as described.

The baseline is 'text-only' while the multi-modal model adds image features AND metadata (product category, price tier, promotions, day-of-week, month). Two modalities are added simultaneously. If the multi-modal model outperforms the baseline, the gain could be from product images, from metadata, or from both — the experiment cannot attribute the gain to the imaging modality specifically. A metadata-only ablation would be needed to isolate the image contribution.

For e-commerce satisfaction prediction, product images represent the seller's current product presentation at review time. However, the image associated with a review may be the image at review submission time, not the image at product purchase time — product images are updated by sellers. If the model learns from current images applied to historical reviews, there is a form of temporal feature leakage where the image at training time does not correspond to what the customer saw when purchasing.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing transformers including image normalization statistics are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-set product image characteristics into the normalization applied during training.

Section 2 describes a random temporal split while Section 6 references a specific temporal month range for validation — these descriptions are mutually contradictory, making the actual split strategy ambiguous.

The multi-modal model adds both image features and metadata simultaneously compared to the text-only baseline; any performance difference cannot be attributed to imaging specifically without a metadata-only ablation.

Product images in e-commerce are updated by sellers over time; if images are associated at training time rather than at the customer's purchase/review time, the image features encode post-purchase information not available to the customer at purchase, creating temporal feature leakage.
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing transformers including image normalization statistics are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, encoding test-set product image characteristics into the normalization applied during training.

Section 2 describes a random temporal split while Section 6 references a specific temporal month range for validation — these descriptions are mutually contradictory, making the actual split strategy ambiguous.

The multi-modal model adds both image features and metadata simultaneously compared to the text-only baseline; any performance difference cannot be attributed to imaging specifically without a metadata-only ablation.

Product images in e-commerce are updated by sellers over time; if images are associated at training time rather than at the customer's purchase/review time, the image features encode post-purchase information not available to the customer at purchase, creating temporal feature leakage.
